elements.sent-2411-001: We show that deciding simulation equivalence and simulation preorder have quadratic lower bounds assuming that the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis holds.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is in line with the best know quadratic upper bounds of simulation equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-003: This means that deciding simulation is inherently quadratic.
elements.sent-2411-004: A typical consequence of this result is that computing simulation equivalence is fundamentally harder than bisimilarity.
elements.sent-2411-005: Commonly, processes are abstractly represented as directed graphs with states and transitions where states or transitions are labelled.
elements.sent-2411-006: One process is simulated by another if each step of the first process can be simulated by the second.
elements.sent-2411-007: Two processes are simulation equivalent if they can both simulate each other.
elements.sent-2411-008: Simulation preorders for the comparison of the behaviour of programs have been defined in \cite{DBLP:conf/ijcai/Milner71 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Algorithms for deciding simulation and simulation preorders were only defined decades later \cite{DBLP:journals/scp/BloomP95,DBLP:conf/focs/HenzingerHK95,DBLP:journals/tocl/BustanG03 and they all have a complexity in terms of the number of states times the number of transitions.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In \cite{DBLP:conf/lics/RanzatoT07,DBLP:conf/cav/GlabbeekP08,DBLP:journals/fuin/CrafaRT11,DBLP:journals/acta/Ranzato14 these algorithms are improved by reducing both the required memory footprint and the time complexity to the number of simulation equivalence classes times the number of transitions, but as the number of equivalence classes can be the number of states this essentially means that all algorithms have quadratic time complexity in terms of the input.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This raises the question whether calculating simulation preorder and simulation equivalence is essentially quadratic.
elements.sent-2411-012: Hitherto, there is no real answer to this question.
elements.sent-2411-013: The only analysis is that determining simulation is at least as hard as bisimilarity \cite{DBLP:conf/concur/KuceraM02 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This follows from the observation that via a polynomial translation of the process graph bisimulation equivalence reduces to simulation equivalence, showing that modulo this transformation simulation can be used to calculate bisimilarity.
elements.sent-2411-015: For bisimilarity there is a quasi-linear lower bound assuming partition refinement is used \cite{DBLP:journals/lmcs/GrooteMV23 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper we provide an answer by showing that if the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH) holds, then determining simulation preorder on deterministic transition systems and simulation equivalence on non-deterministic transition systems must be quadratic in complexity.
elements.sent-2411-017: The Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH) states that satisfiability of a propositional formula with $n$ propositional variables cannot be solved in time $O(2^{\delta n )$ for any $\delta<1$ \cite{impagliazzo2001complexity .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: There is also the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH), also occurring in \cite{impagliazzo2001complexity , which says that determining satisfiability of propositional formulas in conjunctive normal form where each clause has length 3 needs at least time $O(2^{{\epsilon n )$ for some $\epsilon>0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The strong exponential time hypothesis implies the exponential time hypothesis, which in turn implies $P\not=NP$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Both hypotheses ETH and SETH state that in essence no algorithm for satisfiability significantly outperforms brute-force methods.
elements.sent-2411-021: They are especially useful to prove lower bounds for problems within $P$~\cite{oliveira2020finegrained,williams2015hardness .}
elements.sent-2411-022: For us the results in~\cite{oliveira2020finegrained are particularly interesting.}
elements.sent-2411-023: They deal with the problem of determining the non-emptiness of the intersection for $k$ deterministic finite state machines~($k$-DFA-NEI).
elements.sent-2411-024: This problem is defined as as follows.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given $k$ deterministic finite state machines $A_1, \dots, A_k$, each over an alphabet $\Sigma$ and having $n$ states, determine whether \begin{equation}\bigcap_{i=1}^{k} \Lang(A_i) \stackrel{?}{=} \emptyset\end{equation} where $\Lang(A_i)$ is the set of accepted strings by state machine $A_i$.
elements.sent-2411-026: For unbounded $k$ this problem is PSPACE-complete~\cite{kozen1977lower .}
elements.sent-2411-027: If the number of input DFAs $k$ is fixed, this problem can be solved naively in $O(n^k)$ time by constructing the product automaton of the input.
elements.sent-2411-028: Under the assumption ETH, it cannot be solved in $O(n^{o(k) )$ \cite[Prop.. 3]{fernau2017problems .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Furthermore, under the stronger assumption SETH, an algorithm running in $O(n^{k-\epsilon )$ is impossible for any constant $\epsilon > 0$, which is more interesting to us.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We show in a quite straightforward way that if simulation preorder on the initial states of two DFAs, all with $n$ states, can be determined in time $O(n^{2-\epsilon )$, then $2$-DFA-NEI can be solved in time $O(n^{2-\epsilon )$, which would imply that the assumption SETH does not hold.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: As a corollary it follows that computing simulation equivalence for the initial states of two nondeterministic automata is also inherently quadratic.
elements.section-2411-002: SETH implies that DFA-NEI has quadratic complexity
elements.sent-2411-032: In this section we rephrase in more detail that the strong exponential time hypothesis (SETH) implies that calculating the non-emptiness of the intersection of two deterministic finite state machines requires quadratic time.
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, we illustrate by example how a slightly improved algorithm for the non-emptiness of language intersection would mean an exponential improvement for CNF-SAT.
elements.sent-2411-034: More detailed proofs of this construction can be found in \cite[Theorem 7.21]{wehar2017complexity .}
elements.sent-2411-035: We start out with some preliminaries and the common notion of a deterministic finite automaton.
elements.sent-2411-036: An alphabet is a finite set of letters $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-037: A word is a finite sequence of letters over an alphabet where we write $\epsilon$ for the empty sequence.
elements.sent-2411-038: For a number $i\in \Nat$ the set $\Sigma^i$ is the set of all sequences of length $i$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The set $\Sigma^* = \bigcup_{i\in\Nat \Sigma^i$ is the Kleene closure and contains all finite words over $\Sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Given a word $w\in\Sigma^*$ and a position $1 \leq i \leq \lvert w\rvert$, we write $w[i]$ for the $i$-th symbol in $w$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Given a finite number $k$ of DFAs the non-empty intersection problem of DFAs asks whether there is a word which is accepted by all DFAs.
elements.sent-2411-042: More concretely, it contains all tuples of DFAs of which the intersection of the accepted languages is not empty.
elements.sent-2411-043: This problem is well known to be PSPACE-complete~\cite{kozen1977lower .}
elements.sent-2411-044: For a fixed $k$ the problem is naively solvable in $O(n^k)$ by computing the product automata.
elements.sent-2411-045: Surprisingly, it turns out that if this can be computed more efficiently, it also means more efficient algorithms for deciding CNF-SAT~\cite{wehar2017complexity .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Given an $\ell\in\Nat$, the decision problem $\ell$-CNF-SAT is the variant of the boolean satisfiability problem over formulas in conjunctive normal form with at most $\ell$ literals per clause.
elements.sent-2411-048: The computational complexity of deciding $\ell$-CNF-SAT, for increasing values of $\ell$ is studied in~\cite{impagliazzo2001complexity .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Let \begin{equation}s_\ell = \inf\{\delta \mid $\ell$-CNF-SAT is solvable in time  2^{\delta n}\},\end{equation} for each $\ell\in\Nat$, where $\inf$ is the infimum.
elements.sent-2411-050: The exponential time hypothesis (ETH) asserts that $s_3 > 0$, i.e., $3$-CNF-SAT cannot be solved in less than exponential time.
elements.sent-2411-051: The strong exponential time hypothesis~(SETH) asserts that \begin{equation}\lim_{\ell\to \infty} s_\ell = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: Equivalently SETH asserts that for any $\delta < 1$ there is no algorithm solving CNF-SAT that has a runtime of $O(2^{\delta n )$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We consider a CNF-formula $\Phi$ with $m$ clauses $C_1, \dots , C_m$ and an even number $n$ of propositional variables $x_1, \dots , x_n$.
elements.sent-2411-054: We construct the languages $L^\Phi_1, L^\Phi_2 \subseteq \{\bit{0 , \bit{1}\}^*$ that consist of words $w \in \Sigma^{n+m}$ in which the first $n$ letters comprise a bitstring in which for each $1 \leq i\leq n$ the bit $w[i]$ encodes the truth assignment for $x_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The second part of $m$ letters encode a gadget which assigns each clause to either $L^\Phi_1$ or $L^\Phi_2$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We require that $w \in L^\Phi_1$ if and only if $w= w_{\rho b_1\cdots b_m$ for a word $w_{\rho}\in \Sigma^n$ modelling a truth assignment and $b_1,\dots, b_m \in \{\bit{0 , \bit{1}\}$ such that if $b_i = \bit{0}$ then $C_i$ is satisfied by the assignment to some variable $x_1, \dots, x_{\frac{1 {2 n}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Similarly, $w_\rho b_1\cdots b_m \in L^\Phi_2$ if and only for all $1\leq i \leq m$ if $b_i = 1$ then $C_i$ is satisfied by the assignment of some variable $x_{\frac{1 {2 n+1 , \dots, x_n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In Fig.~\ref{fig:dfa-sat the minimal automata that accepts the languages $L_1^{\Psi}$ and $L_2^{\Psi}$ are given.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Observe that, given a formula $\Phi$, for each truth assignment $w_\rho \in \{0,1\}^n$ there is an extension of $w_C \in \{0,1\}^m$ such that $w_\rho w_c \in L^\Phi_1 \cap L^\Phi_2$ if and only if $w_\rho$ models a satisfying assignment for $\Phi$.
elements.sent-2411-061: This means the intersection $L^\Phi_1 \cap L^\Phi_2$ is not empty if and only if there is a satisfying assignment for $\Phi$.
elements.sent-2411-062: This allows us to prove satisfiability of $\Phi$ by constructing $L^\Phi_1$ and $L^\Phi_2$ and show that their intersection is not empty.
elements.sent-2411-063: Now note that the minimal DFAs that accepts $L^\Phi_1$ and $L^\Phi_2$ both contain at most $m n 2^{\frac{1 {2 n}$ states.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Assume that we can calculate language intersection on two graphs with $N$ states in time $O(N^{2-\epsilon )$, then we can construct $L^\Phi_1$ and $L^\Phi_2$, and calculate their intersection in time $O(n^{2-\epsilon m^{2-\epsilon 2^{n(1-\frac{1 {2}\epsilon) )$ determining CNF-SAT.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: But this refutes the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis, saying that for CNF-SAT cannot be solved $2^{\delta n}$ for any $\delta<1$.
elements.section-2411-003: SETH implies simulation has quadratic complexity
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section we show that determining simulation preorder on deterministic, and simulation equivalence on non-deterministic transition systems is necessarily quadratic, assuming SETH is valid.
elements.sent-2411-067: Simulation is typically defined on labelled transition systems which divert slightly from DFAs.
elements.sent-2411-068: The LTS $L$ is called deterministic iff there is at most one outgoing transition for every combination of state $s\in S$ and action label $a\in Act$, i.e. $\lvert \{ s' \mid s\pijl{a s' \}\rvert \leq 1$ for every $(s,a) \in S\times \Sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Given an LTS $M_1 = (S_1, Act_1, \pijl{ _1, s_1)$ and two states $s,t\in S_1$ we say $s$ is simulated by $t$ iff $s \simby t$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Given a second LTS $M_2 = (S_2, Act_2, \pijl{ _2,s_2)$, we write $M_1 \simby M_2$ iff $s_1\simby s_2$ in the combined LTS $M = (S_1 \cup S_2, Act_1 \cup Act_2, \pijl{ _1 \cup \pijl{ _2, s_1)$, where we assume w.l.o.g.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We say two states $s,t$ are simulation equivalent, written $s\simeq t$, iff they simulate each other.
elements.sent-2411-072: A simulation relation which is symmetric is called a bisimulation relation.
elements.sent-2411-073: We introduce a mapping $\alpha$ from automata to deterministic LTSs such that given two DFAs $A_1, A_2$, for the projected deterministic LTSs $M_1 = \alpha(A_1), M_2 = \alpha(A_2)$ it holds that $M_1 \simby M_2$ if and only if $\Lang(A_1)\subseteq \Lang(A_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Informally, the mapping $\alpha$ maintains the same transition structure in the deterministic LTSs but adds one state and action label that encodes the accepting states of the automata.
elements.sent-2411-075: More formally, given a DFA $A=(Q,\Sigma, \delta, F, q_0)$, we define the deterministic LTS $\alpha(A) = (Q \cup \{\top\}, \Sigma \cup \{\checkmark\}, \pijl{ , q_0)$, with a fresh symbols $\top\not\in Q,\checkmark\not\in \Sigma$, and where the transition relation $\pijl{}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}{\pijl{}} = \{ (q,a,\delta(q,a)) \mid for each  q,a\in Q\times \Sigma\}\, \cup\, \{(q,\checkmark, \top) \mid for each q\in F\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}{\pijl{}} = \{ (q,a,\delta(q,a)) \mid for each  q,a\in Q\times \Sigma\}\, \cup\, \{(q,\checkmark, \top) \mid for each q\in F\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-076: As the construction above is quite straightforward, it is easy to see that we have the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-077: This allows us to compute $2$-DFA-NEI by translating the involved DFAs to deterministic LTSs using $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Not that deciding $\Lang(A) \cap \Lang(B) = \emptyset$ is equivalent to deciding $\Lang(A) \subseteq \Sigma^*\setminus \Lang(B)$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The DFA $\overline{B}$ is the complement of $B$, e.g. with all accepting and non-accepting states swapped, such that $\Lang(\overline{B ) = \Sigma^* \setminus \Lang(B)$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Now by Theorem~\ref{thm:subset-sim it holds that $\alpha(A) \simby \alpha\left(\overline{B}\right)$ if and only if $\Lang(A) \cap \Lang(B) = \emptyset$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Computing $\alpha(A),\alpha(\overline{B )$ can be done in $O(n)$, and hence any $f(n)$ algorithm for similarity could be translated to $2-DFA-NEI$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: This immediately translates to the following corollary.
elements.sent-2411-083: Meaning that deciding simulation equivalence on deterministic structures can be done in almost linear time~\cite{hopcroft1971linear,DBLP:conf/coco/Fischer72,tarjan1975efficiency , where `almost' refers to a multiplicative factor with the inverse Ackermann's function.}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, using one non-deterministic transition a faster than quadratic algorithm for simulation equivalence on LTSs in general violates SETH.
elements.sent-2411-085: In order to see that deciding simulation equivalence is also computationally equivalent to deciding $2-DFA-NEI$ we reduce from deciding similarity on deterministic LTSs by adding one non-deterministic state.
elements.sent-2411-086: Given LTSs $M_1 = (S_1, \Sigma, \pijl{ _1, s_0)$ and $M_2 = (S_2, \Sigma, \pijl{ _2, t_0)$, we construct the LTS $M = (S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \{s,t\}, \pijl{ _1 \cup \pijl{ _2 \cup \{(s ,a, s_0), (s,a,t_0), (t,a,t_0)\}$ with two fresh states $s,t\not\in S_1 \cup S_2$ for some $a\in\Sigma$.. See Fig.~\ref{fig:M .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Now in $M$ it holds that $s \simeq t \iff s_0 \simby t_0$.
elements.sent-2411-088: If simulation equivalence for LTSs is solvable in $O(n^{2-\epsilon )$, then, using this construction, similarity is solvable in $O(n^{2-\epsilon )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: In that case it follows from Corollary~\ref{cor:similarity that SETH is false.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The proof technique employed in this paper appears to be quite universal.
elements.sent-2411-091: It promises to be useable to show fine-grained, i.e., sub-exponential, lower bounds for a much wider classes of problems in the domain of process theory and model checking.
elements.sent-2411-092: Unfortunately, as yet, we were not able to do so.. \bibliographystyle{plainurl.}
